%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# Discrete-Time Impulse Function \(\delta[n]\)

> **Section Objective:** Learn the discrete-time impulse \(\delta[n]\) and how shifting creates \(\delta[n-m]\).

---

## Concepts In This Section

- unit impulse sequence
- Kronecker delta
- shifted impulse sequence

## 1. Unit Impulse Sequence

The discrete-time unit impulse \(\delta[n]\) is the discrete counterpart of an impulse signal. Unlike the continuous-time Dirac delta — which is a distribution, not a function — \(\delta[n]\) is simply an ordinary sequence of numbers.

It has exactly one nonzero value: it equals **1** at the single integer index \(n=0\), and equals **0** at every other integer.

### QUICK EXAMPLES

- \(\delta[0] = 1\) — the only nonzero sample
- \(\delta[2] = 0\) — any index other than zero gives zero
- \(\delta[-3] = 0\) — same rule applies for negative indices

#### Note

No distribution theory is needed here. \(\delta[n]\) is just a sequence with one spike at the origin.

## 2. Shifted impulse sequence

**Reading the formula:**

- \(n\) is an integer time index (e.g., \(n = \ldots, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, \ldots\))
- The output is **1 only at index \(n=0\)**; it is 0 everywhere else
- This is also called the **Kronecker delta**

**When to use this:**
- Any problem that says "unit impulse", "Kronecker delta", or "sample selector"
- Any sequence described as having exactly one nonzero sample at the origin

**Exam trigger:** A sequence with exactly one nonzero sample of height 1 located at \(n=0\).

**Common misuse:** Treating \(\delta[n]\) like the continuous-time Dirac delta (infinitely tall, zero width). The discrete-time impulse is simply the number 1 at one index — nothing exotic.

$$\delta[n] = \begin{cases}1, & n=0 \\ 0, & n\neq 0\end{cases}$$


## 2. Shifted Impulse Sequence

The shifted impulse \(\delta[n-m]\) moves the single nonzero sample from \(n=0\) to \(n=m\). The shape is identical — still exactly one spike of height 1 — but its location changes.

### CONCRETE EXAMPLE

For \(\delta[n-3]\):
- The impulse equals **1 at \(n=3\)**
- It equals **0 at every other integer**

### EXAM SHORTCUT

To find where any shifted impulse is nonzero, **set the bracket argument equal to zero**:

$$n - m = 0 \implies n = m$$

### COMMON MISTAKE

Students often place \(\delta[n-m]\) at \(n = -m\). This is **wrong**. Solving \(n - m = 0\) gives \(n = +m\), not \(-m\). The minus sign inside the bracket does not flip the location to negative.

$$\delta[n-m] = \begin{cases}1, & n=m \\ 0, & n\neq m\end{cases}$$
***Reading the formula:**

- \(m\) is the **shift amount** — it is also the exact index where the impulse appears
- The impulse is nonzero only when the argument \(n - m\) equals zero, i.e., when \(n = m\)

**When to use this:**
- Any expression of the form \(\delta[n - m]\) with a shifted argument
- Finding which sample of a sequence is "selected" by the impulse

**Exam trigger:** Any \(\delta[\cdot]\) with a non-trivial argument — solve the argument equal to zero.

**Common misuse:** Reading \(n - m\) as a shift to \(-m\). Always solve \(n - m = 0\) explicitly to get \(n = m\).*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="example_support" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Unit impulse: \(\delta[n] = \begin{cases}1, & n=0 \\ 0, & n\neq 0\end{cases}\) — exactly one nonzero sample of height 1 at the origin.
- Shifted impulse: \(\delta[n-m] = \begin{cases}1, & n=m \\ 0, & n\neq m\end{cases}\) — the spike moves to index \(n=m\).
- Exam trigger: set the impulse argument equal to zero (\(n - m = 0\)) to find the nonzero index \(n = m\).

*Next, this impulse becomes a building block for representing and analyzing discrete-time signals.*
